sources.citation: Olson, D. H. (2000). Circumplex model of marital and family systems. Journal of Family Therapy, 22(2), 144–167.
sources.relevance: Foundational model for measuring family cohesion and adaptability; directly informs the conceptualization of family solidarity and relational quality.
sources.key_finding: Family cohesion—defined as the emotional bonding among family members—is a core dimension predicting family functioning and well-being.

sources.citation: Bengtson, V. L., & Roberts, R. E. L. (1991). Intergenerational solidarity in aging families: An example of formal theory construction. Journal of Marriage and the Family, 53(4), 856–870.
sources.relevance: Develops the intergenerational solidarity model that captures affectual, associational, and consensual dimensions of family bonds, closely aligned with this construct.
sources.key_finding: Family solidarity is a multidimensional construct encompassing emotional closeness, shared values, and frequency of contact across generations.

sources.citation: Esteinou, R. (2008). La familia nuclear en México: Lecturas de su modernidad. Siglos XVI al XX. CIESAS.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the evolution of family cohesion and relational quality in the Mexican context, providing culturally specific insights.
sources.key_finding: Mexican families exhibit strong affective bonds rooted in extended kinship networks, though modernization has introduced new tensions in family cohesion.

sources.citation: Rivera Heredia, M. E., & Andrade Palos, P. (2010). Escala de evaluación de las relaciones intrafamiliares (E.R.I.). Uaricha, Revista de Psicología, 14, 12–29.
sources.relevance: Develops and validates a scale for measuring intra-family relationships in Mexican populations, directly relevant to operationalizing family cohesion quality.
sources.key_finding: The scale identifies three factors—union and support, expression, and difficulties—that capture the quality of family relationships among Mexican respondents.

sources.citation: Inglehart, R., & Norris, P. (2003). Rising tide: Gender equality and cultural change around the world. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Examines the shift from traditional to progressive values regarding family structure and gender roles across societies, including Latin America.
sources.key_finding: Modernization drives a cultural shift from traditional family ideologies emphasizing male breadwinner norms toward more egalitarian gender attitudes.

sources.citation: Esteinou, R. (2004). El surgimiento de la familia nuclear en México. Estudios de Historia Novohispana, 31, 99–136.
sources.relevance: Traces the historical emergence and persistence of traditional family ideology in Mexico, providing essential cultural context.
sources.key_finding: The nuclear family ideal in Mexico is deeply intertwined with Catholic moral frameworks and colonial legacies that continue to shape contemporary attitudes.

sources.citation: Barker, G. T. (2005). Dying to be men: Youth, masculinity and social exclusion. Routledge.
sources.relevance: Discusses how traditional family ideology intersects with masculinity norms in Latin American contexts, relevant to gender role items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Traditional family ideologies reinforce rigid gender roles that have measurable consequences for family violence, women's labor force participation, and child-rearing practices.

sources.citation: García, B., & de Oliveira, O. (2011). Family changes and public policies in Latin America. Annual Review of Sociology, 37, 593–611.
sources.relevance: Reviews how attitudes toward family structure and gender roles in Latin America are shifting and how policy responds to these changes.
sources.key_finding: Despite rapid demographic and social change, traditional family ideologies remain influential in shaping public policy and individual behavior in Mexico and Latin America.

sources.citation: Rocha-Sánchez, T. E., & Díaz-Loving, R. (2005). Cultura de género: La brecha ideológica entre hombres y mujeres. Anales de Psicología, 21(1), 42–49.
sources.relevance: Directly measures gender ideology gaps in Mexico, closely aligned with the conservative-to-progressive dimension captured by this construct.
sources.key_finding: Significant ideological gaps exist between men and women in Mexico regarding traditional versus egalitarian views of family and gender roles.

causal_relationships.related_construct: attitudes toward women's labor force participation
acceptance of diverse family forms (same-sex unions, single parenthood)

sources.citation: Oppenheimer, V. K. (1988). A theory of marriage timing. American Journal of Sociology, 94(3), 563–591.
sources.relevance: Provides a theoretical framework for understanding instrumental versus affective motivations in partner selection and union formation.
sources.key_finding: Economic considerations and career uncertainty significantly shape the timing and motivations for marriage, alongside affective factors.

sources.citation: Quilodrán, J. (2001). Un siglo de matrimonio en México. El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Comprehensive analysis of marriage patterns in Mexico over the 20th century, directly relevant to understanding union formation motivations in the Mexican context.
sources.key_finding: Union formation in Mexico has diversified from predominantly religious marriage toward cohabitation and civil unions, reflecting shifting motivations from institutional to relational.

sources.citation: Cherlin, A. J. (2004). The deinstitutionalization of American marriage. Journal of Marriage and Family, 66(4), 848–861.
sources.relevance: Theorizes the shift from institutional to companionate to individualized marriage, relevant to understanding the affective-instrumental dimension.
sources.key_finding: Marriage has shifted from an institution organized around economic production and social obligation to one centered on personal fulfillment and emotional satisfaction.

sources.citation: Solís, P., & Ferraris, S. (2014). Nuevo siglo, ¿nuevas pautas de formación y disolución de uniones? In C. Rabell (Ed.), Los mexicanos: Un balance del cambio demográfico (pp. 269–305). Fondo de Cultura Económica.
sources.relevance: Examines contemporary union formation patterns in Mexico, including the rise of cohabitation and its motivations.
sources.key_finding: Cohabitation has increased substantially in Mexico, particularly among younger and lower-income populations, driven by both economic constraints and changing values.

sources.citation: Amato, P. R. (2000). The consequences of divorce for adults and children. Journal of Marriage and Family, 62(4), 1269–1287.
sources.relevance: Comprehensive review of the causes and consequences of family dissolution, directly relevant to the construct of perceived family vulnerability.
sources.key_finding: Divorce is associated with declines in psychological well-being, economic resources, and social support for both adults and children.

sources.citation: Castro, R., & Casique, I. (2010). Violencia en el hogar y desigualdad de género. In R. Castro & I. Casique (Eds.), Estudios sobre cultura, género y violencia contra las mujeres (pp. 69–104). UNAM-CRIM.
sources.relevance: Examines domestic violence and family conflict in the Mexican context, directly aligned with items on violence and family breakdown.
sources.key_finding: Family violence in Mexico is strongly linked to gender inequality, economic stress, and patriarchal family structures.

sources.citation: Ojeda, N. (2009). Matrimonio y unión libre en la percepción de adolescentes mexicanos radicados en Tijuana. Papeles de Población, 15(60), 41–63.
sources.relevance: Explores perceptions of family instability, divorce, and union dissolution among Mexican populations.
sources.key_finding: Perceptions of family instability are shaped by direct exposure to parental conflict and divorce, as well as broader socioeconomic conditions.

sources.citation: Frías, S. M., & Angel, R. J. (2005). The risk of partner violence among low-income Hispanic subgroups. Journal of Marriage and Family, 67(3), 552–564.
sources.relevance: Analyzes risk factors for intimate partner violence among Hispanic populations, relevant to the conflict and violence dimensions of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Economic deprivation, low education, and cultural factors interact to increase the risk of partner violence in Hispanic families.

sources.citation: Grusec, J. E., & Hastings, P. D. (Eds.). (2015). Handbook of socialization: Theory and research (2nd ed.). Guilford Press.
sources.relevance: Comprehensive reference on family socialization processes, including the mechanisms by which parents transmit values to children.
sources.key_finding: Family socialization is a bidirectional process in which parental values, discipline practices, and modeling shape children's internalization of norms and moral development.

sources.citation: Rohan, M. J., & Zanna, M. P. (1996). Value transmission in families. In C. Seligman, J. M. Olson, & M. P. Zanna (Eds.), The psychology of values: The Ontario Symposium (Vol. 8, pp. 253–276). Lawrence Erlbaum.
sources.relevance: Directly examines the mechanisms and effectiveness of intergenerational value transmission within families.
sources.key_finding: Value transmission is most effective when parents are consistent, warm, and when children perceive parental values accurately.

sources.citation: Esteinou, R. (2015). Autonomía adolescente y apoyo y control parental en familias indígenas mexicanas. Revista Latinoamericana de Ciencias Sociales, Niñez y Juventud, 13(2), 749–766.
sources.relevance: Examines parental socialization practices in Mexican families, including indigenous contexts, directly relevant to values transmission in the Mexican setting.
sources.key_finding: Mexican families employ culturally specific socialization strategies that balance autonomy and control, with significant variation across indigenous and non-indigenous populations.

sources.citation: Schwartz, S. H. (2012). An overview of the Schwartz theory of basic values. Online Readings in Psychology and Culture, 2(1), 1–20.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework for understanding which values are transmitted and how they are structured, relevant to interpreting the values items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Basic human values form a circular motivational structure, and family socialization is a primary mechanism through which cultural value priorities are reproduced across generations.

sources.citation: Alduncin, E. (2004). Los valores de los mexicanos. Banamex.
sources.relevance: Empirical study of Mexican values including those transmitted through family, directly relevant to the Mexican public opinion context.
sources.key_finding: Family remains the most important institution for value transmission in Mexico, with religion, respect, and hard work among the most frequently cited values passed from parents to children.

causal_relationships.related_construct: attitudes toward corruption and rule of law
gender role attitudes in next generation
youth risk behaviors and prosocial development
